items.myth: A random variable must be random in the sense of changing every time you look at it.
items.reality: A random variable is a fixed function X(ω) on outcomes; 'random' means the outcome ω is uncertain before the experiment, not that X itself is undefined.
items.example: Let X = 1 if a coin flip is heads, 0 if tails. X is a well-defined function; only the outcome ω is unknown before flipping.

items.myth: If f(x) > 1, it cannot be a valid PDF.
items.reality: PDF values are densities, not probabilities; f(x) can exceed 1 as long as the total integral over the real line equals 1.
items.example: Uniform(0, 0.5) has f(x) = 2 on (0, 0.5) and integrates to 1—valid despite density 2.

items.myth: Softmax probabilities are always calibrated estimates of P(Y = k).
items.reality: Softmax outputs form a valid PMF (non-negative, sum to 1) but may be overconfident or underconfident unless the model is well-calibrated on held-out data.
items.example: A spam filter may output p(spam) = 0.99 for 99% of flagged emails but only 60% are actually spam—valid PMF, poor calibration.
